current_fact.fact_text: On April 10, 1809, the War of the Fifth Coalition began just as earlier coalitions had failed to topple Napoleon—proof that Europe was still rethinking the balance of power. Each new alliance brought fresh strategies, rivalries, and shifting loyalties across the map.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

current_fact.match_reason: Shifting coalitions and campaigns across Europe mirror a sonic journey of many styles and regions; this episode’s "destination" theme and varied musical territories echo an era of constantly redrawn maps.

queue.fact_text: On April 10, 1979, the same tornado outbreak that devastated Wichita Falls also produced multiple powerful twisters across Oklahoma and Texas, earning the day a place among the most destructive U.S. tornado events. Photographs from the time show entire city blocks scoured to concrete slabs.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

queue.match_reason: The swirling, spinning violence of multiple tornadoes connects metaphorically to the revolving disco ball and spiraling grooves of cosmic disco—storms and spin, one destructive, one ecstatic.

queue.fact_text: On April 10, 2016’s Afghanistan earthquake, seismic waves raced silently beneath borders, reminding us that tectonic plates ignore human lines on maps. Sensors across India, Pakistan, Tajikistan, and beyond recorded the same deep rumble from a shared, restless Earth.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A quake that crosses borders suits an open-format mix designed to drift "beyond the expected"—both are about unseen forces moving underneath rigid lines and categories.

queue.fact_text: On April 10, 1970, as Paul McCartney announced his departure, many fans felt as though an era ended overnight—but The Beatles’ legacy continued to echo in every corner of modern music. Their breakup marked the moment when four young men from Liverpool stopped sharing a stage and started reshaping sound in four different directions at once.

episode.title: Sedna FM #03 - Beautiful as You Feel
episode.description: An episode about self-acceptance and inner beauty. Music that reminds us we are beautiful as we feel, embracing our authentic selves. Warm, uplifting sounds that celebrate individuality.
songs: The The - this is the day
DCTL - Deep Tannin f(eat Rafael Leafar)

queue.match_reason: The end of The Beatles forced each member to stand alone as an individual artist; this episode’s focus on self-acceptance and feeling "beautiful as you feel" echoes that shift from collective identity to personal authenticity.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 837, Halley's Comet made its closest recorded pass by Earth, swooping in to just 0.0342 astronomical units—about 5.1 million kilometers away. Medieval skywatchers saw an enormous, ghostly streak across the heavens, unaware they were witnessing a visitor that returns roughly every 76 years.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A comet skimming past Earth is a literal "space shift", mirroring the episode's theme of reimagining emptiness as creative cosmic space and its energising, forward-moving sound.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1815, Mount Tambora began an eruption so colossal it blasted away the top of the volcano and altered the planet’s climate. The ash veiled the skies, cooling Earth enough to trigger crop failures and the eerie “Year Without a Summer” that haunted 1816.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A volcanic blast that dimmed the sun and warped the seasons echoes the episode's focus on storms, rain, and finding hope through long, unsettling climate shifts.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1991, a rare tropical storm formed in the South Atlantic near Angola, in a region once thought almost immune to such cyclones. Its appearance forced meteorologists to rethink how ocean temperatures, wind patterns, and climate make storms possible in "impossible" places.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A storm in an unexpected ocean mirrors the episode’s theme of encountering the unfamiliar while traveling and letting surprising environments shift your perspective.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1979, a ferocious tornado tore through Wichita Falls, Texas, in the Red River Valley outbreak, killing 42 people and flattening entire neighborhoods. Its immense damage path—more than a mile wide in places—helped spur better forecasting, warning systems, and building standards for tornado-prone regions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The raw, explosive energy of a mile-wide tornado fits the episode’s "electric shock" intensity and high-voltage rock guitars, like a storm translated into sound.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 2016, a magnitude 6.6 earthquake shook a broad swath of Central and South Asia, from Afghanistan’s mountains to cities in India and Pakistan. The quake reminded scientists how deeply the collision of tectonic plates under the Himalayas still reshapes the land—and lives—above.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A sudden tectonic jolt that reshapes entire regions parallels the episode’s focus on disruptive moments that force us to adapt, rethink, and gradually rebuild stronger foundations.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1963, the nuclear-powered submarine USS Thresher sank during deep-diving tests, taking 129 sailors and civilians with it. The tragedy led the U.S. Navy to overhaul its safety culture and launch the SUBSAFE program, radically improving the reliability of submarines operating in the planet’s most crushing depths.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A submarine lost in the deep ocean resonates with an episode built around diving, immersion, and the mysterious pull of what lies beneath the surface.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1912, the RMS Titanic slipped away from Southampton on her luxury-packed maiden voyage, hailed as an engineering marvel and, famously, "practically unsinkable". Within days, that confidence would collapse into one of history’s most haunting maritime disasters.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: Titanic’s fateful voyage is a grand, tragic version of an ocean journey, connecting naturally with an episode recorded on a sailing boat and steeped in life at sea.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1866, Henry Bergh founded the American Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals in New York City. At a time when horses were beaten in the streets and animals had almost no legal protection, his work helped launch a global movement for animal welfare.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

published.match_reason: An early stand against cruelty and for compassion pairs with an episode centered on powerful, empathetic voices that challenge and protect—soul as a vehicle for justice and care.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1724, Johann Sebastian Bach led the first performance of his Easter cantata "Erfreut euch, ihr Herzen" in Leipzig. The piece weaves trumpet fanfares, choir, and soloists into a luminous celebration that helped define the soundscape of Lutheran Easter.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: Bach’s radiant Easter cantata shares a spiritual, uplifting quality with the episode’s sunlit, jazz-infused sense of renewal and celebration.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1970, Paul McCartney quietly announced he was leaving The Beatles, effectively ending a band that had transformed popular music in just a decade. The breakup shocked fans, but it also freed four musicians to explore radically different solo identities.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

published.match_reason: McCartney leaving The Beatles is the ultimate act of shedding one identity for many new ones, perfectly fitting an episode about alter-egos and artistic reinvention—plus it even includes a McCartney track.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1973, Invicta International Airlines Flight 435 crashed into a forested hillside near Basel, Switzerland, after getting disoriented in a snowstorm, killing 108 people. The disaster pushed aviation to refine approaches in poor visibility and improve cockpit procedures in complex terrain.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: A tragic crash in snow and cloud contrasts with the episode’s gentle, grounding downtempo mood—an imagined soft landing and slowed pace in response to the risks of speed and disorientation.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1972, U.S. B-52 bombers resumed heavy raids on North Vietnam for the first time since 1967, unleashing high-altitude carpet bombing over cities and supply routes. The deafening missions symbolized the war’s escalating air power and the deepening human cost on the ground.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: High-altitude bombing and protest songs both turn on explosions and resistance; this episode’s explicitly political, anti-war energy matches the intensity and moral weight of the B-52 raids.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1809, Austrian forces invaded Bavaria, igniting the War of the Fifth Coalition against Napoleon’s France. The campaign would see rapid maneuvers, surprise battles, and the continued evolution of mass conscript armies reshaping European warfare.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: Coalition warfare and invasions carry a charge of confrontation and defiance, reflected musically in an episode powered by distorted guitars, refusal, and taking up space without permission.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1796, a surprise Austrian attack at the Battle of Voltri opened Napoleon Bonaparte’s Italian campaign, a whirlwind of battles that would crush the First Coalition within a year. The campaign showcased his talent for rapid movement, psychological warfare, and turning small advantages into decisive victories.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: The opening battle of a decisive campaign mirrors Sedna FM’s own "where it all began" first episode—both are explosive starting points that set the tone for everything that follows.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1814, Allied forces under the Duke of Wellington fought their way into Toulouse, defeating Marshal Soult’s French army in one of the last major battles of the Napoleonic Wars. Ironically, fierce fighting raged even though Napoleon had already abdicated days earlier, news that had not yet arrived.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

published.match_reason: A battle fought through literal gunpowder smoke and fiery resistance aligns with an episode themed around "fog and fire"—contrasting confusion, heat, and the emotional climate of a country divided.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1868, British and Indian troops defeated Emperor Tewodros II’s forces at Arogee in Abyssinia after a brutal clash in the Ethiopian highlands. Despite overwhelming casualties for the defenders, the campaign is remembered both for its military precision and for the looting of priceless Ethiopian manuscripts and treasures.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: A campaign in the rugged mountains of Ethiopia connects to an episode about desert peoples, resistance, and music forged in colonially scarred landscapes of Africa.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1919, the Makhnovshchina held the Third Regional Congress of Peasants, Workers and Insurgents in Huliaipole, Ukraine. In the chaos of civil war, they attempted a rare experiment: a stateless, self-managed society run through assemblies rather than rulers.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: An anarchist congress building a society from below pairs with an episode about a fiercely political artist; both are about challenging power structures and imagining new forms of community.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1545, the mining town of Villa Imperial de Carlos V—modern Potosí in Bolivia—was founded after the discovery of Cerro Rico’s immense silver deposits. For centuries, its mountain fueled Spanish wealth and global trade at the cost of brutal labor and countless Indigenous and enslaved lives, earning the saying "worth a Potosí" to mean unimaginable riches.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: Potosí’s silver empire is inseparable from Spanish colonial history; an episode on flamenco—Spain’s own deep, often painful musical heritage—echoes the cultural legacy of that wealth and suffering.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1941, the Axis powers proclaimed the Independent State of Croatia, a fascist puppet regime aligned with Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy. Its brutal policies and ethnic persecutions left scars that still shape memories and politics in the Balkans today.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

published.match_reason: A fascist puppet state in the Balkans is a stark example of dangerous ideologies; the episode’s invitation to "think again" and its Balkan musical thread encourage re-examining inherited narratives and national myths.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1717, Robert Walpole resigned from the British government, triggering the "Whig Split" that fractured the dominant political party for several years. Out of that turbulence, Walpole would eventually return and consolidate power as Britain’s first de facto prime minister.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: Walpole’s resignation and return show how systems can fracture and then reconfigure; the episode similarly examines stepping away from a dominant system (phones, constant connectivity) to rethink how we relate to power structures in our lives.

published.fact_text: On April 10, 1809, the War of the Fifth Coalition began just as earlier coalitions had failed to topple Napoleon—proof that Europe was still rethinking the balance of power. Each new alliance brought fresh strategies, rivalries, and shifting loyalties across the map.
